replies.author: @paulswarthout9967
replies.text:
A BTU is a BTU is a BTU. The law of conservation of energy applies to the argument. The amount of heat produced by a smallish flame is the same whether you're heating the air (convection) or heating the things around the space (radiant). The BTU output of a candle flame, burning paraffin wax is only about 9BTUs. That's just 65% of the BTUs produced with an electric 4-watt nightlight bulb. A candle burning soy wax only produces around 6BTUs. A typical candle isn't going to create any kind of meaningful heat.

However, with that said, Robert is using vegetable oil, which is more closely associated with soy than with paraffin. He also has a flame which is at least 1.5" in height and a wick up to 1/2" in diameter. It's going to output orders of magnitude more heat than a typical candle. For comparison, a typical hurricane lantern with a half inch flat wick can output as much as 900BTUs/hour when burning kerosene. That kind of heating can definitely warm a small room with radiant heat.

In 2020, I kept a 125CF polypropylene (PE) greenhouse at 68F when the outside temperature was -10F (a 78F temperature differential on opposite sides of flimsy plastic!!) using a 1500watt oil filled radiator space heater and (3) 1-K kerosene hurricane lanterns to supplement the electric space heater.

The argument as to whether a terra cotta pot heater can work or not, has more to do with the size of the flame than anything. A typical candle or tea light candle just doesn't output enough BTUs to accomplish much of anything. The wick is barely a 1/16 of an inch in diameter, and the flame is not much more than 3/8" tall. But if you create your own candles -- vegetable oil, kerosene, paraffin oil, diesel fuel or even vegetable shortening -- with a quarter inch round wick or larger, or multiple quarter inch round wicks, then that terra cotta pot heater will work like a charm. As BTU levels rise, so too will fuel consumption. You'll get the most bang from your buck with diesel fuel, once you overcome its tendency to gel in cold weather.

replies.author: @onelawforall
replies.text: I watched one video just recently where they explained that what happens, namely with the paraffin wax tealight candles, is when you put enough candles together (in this example he had four) in certain terracotta pot setups, it can eventually gasify the wax causing a flash fire and in some cases causing the terracotta pot to explode.  Paraffin is, as I understand it, oil based. So we want to design our heat source to not cause any given choice of fuel source to exceed a, shall we call it, a safe operating temperature.  Oil fires cannot be put out with water, so having a carbon felt or sand or something to suffocate an oil fire is good to have on hand.  Most commenters suggested increasing the draft or airflow to limit the high temperature of heat that the pot would trap.

author: @SoWackyPaddy
text: Robert - love  your channel. One reason the shiny, steel pot got so hot is that the polished metal has a low emissivity, meaning that at a given temperature it does not radiate as much. Much of the heat will therefore remain in the metal until it convects away through natural convection. Maybe a follow on video would be on using pans or pots with different emissivities. Cheers.

replies.author: @Paxmax
replies.text: This is basically what I wanted to add, if yer building an radiating heater, shiny is bad, you want to radiate, so one must chose a proper surface for that. Clay probably has better emissivity, using the graphite cover trick presented by Robert in another video is a good idea. Graphite, being black and somewhat matte should be a good radiative surface treatment. Graphite is also such a thin layer it doesn't pose much of an isolator.

author: @oldschoolcfi3833
text: I think regardless of the method of heat delivery, radiation, convection, or conduction, the Amount of heat available and the losses in the system determines how much warming will occur.  A average candle produces about 80 watts of energy, mostly heat, and a bit of light.  So no matter how you spread the energy, you're still trying to heat a room with a very small power source.  Thermodynamics give us the isobaric specific heat of air is  0.0002793 kWh/(kg*K) (or about 3watt-hours per kg per degree C temperature change).   Air density is usually 1.2Kg/m3.   So assuming a room that is a cube 2.5meters on a side,  that's 15.6 cubic meters of air, or about 18.75kgs of air.  To raise the air temperature 1 degree would take  15.6 kg * 3whs or about 56 watts delivered over an hour. So yes a candle under a pot, or just sitting in a dish, would heat a room, as long as that room was very very well insulated, as any losses through the rooms walls, doors, or windows would consume the heat input rapidly!!

author: @TimelyTinkerer
text: I've never bought into the "candle and plant pot heater" idea for heating a room.  I base my schetical thoughts on my understanding of physics. There is a set amount of fuel within the candle body and therefore a set amount of calories/joules of heat it can output. By lighting a candle you are changing that fuel into heat. This fuel is finite and burning it in any way will theoretically  produce the same amount of heat.  Putting a plant pot over it will not mysteriously make the candle produce more heat, only help it to produce a different kind of heat. I do agree with your explanation that the radiant heat will make the local area to the heater feel warmer and may therefore make people believe this is producing heat to warm a room faster, where in reality its a perceived phenomenon that its producing more heat. I would argue that the candle is producing the same calorific output however it is burnt as the fuel available only has a set amount of calories/joules within it.   Burning it freestanding without a plant pot would surely mean it still produces the exact amount of heat.  I do think, however, that the idea is worth pursuing to devise a "thing" that can produce heat using abundant (and probably hard to utilize) fuels like waste vegetable oil. I love the "forever wick" and can see its potential in creating a heater in this manner. I'm not saying the idea is total  bunkum, just that the amount of heat produced will always be the same.

replies.author: @harrymills2770
replies.text:
I've changed my mind back and forth on the terra cotta heater.

I think it captures some of the heat that would otherwise just go straight up to the ceiling and made a spider up there think it was Chinook season.  It doesn't add one erg of energy given off by the flame, but it re-directs some of that energy sideways to where people are.  It'll feel much warmer next to it with a metal or ceramic radiator absorbing and re-radiating the heat to the side.  

At first, I thought it might be the heat mass of the clay pot extending the release time of the heat, and it would continue to give off heat after the candle was extinguished, but it'd only have as much heat stored up as its heat capacity times its mass would allow, and it's not really that much mass, and the temperatures achieved aren't really that high.

You need a lot of mass and a lot of heat put into that mass to do a whole lot after the flame goes out.  I just think there's more to the re-radiation of some of the heat, laterally, is what makes it out-perform a burner, alone.

The Roman hypocaust was probably the first example of that principle being deliberately put into practice, convection driven underfloor radiant heating, then later the mass stoves of eastern europe.

author: @MichaelAckerman
text:
Two ideas to minimize convection heat and increase radiant heat:

1) use a counterflow heat exchanger to heat the oxygen-rich intake air with the oxygen-poor exhaust air.

2) Use "cold mirrors" to reflect the non-heating wavelengths back to the flame. Cold mirrors and hot mirrors are made with special materials to selectively reflect light wavelengths.

author: @martynharveythepoet5114
text: I note a couple of people saying that, at the end of the day, the heating effect produced is limited by the fact that a candle/wick is only going to produce a specific/limited number of BTUs... and you can't produce any more heat than what can be achieved by releasing the energy from those BTUs. However, the whole point of the Plant Pot Heater idea is that, if you just burn a candle (or whatever) those BTUs get converted into light and heat: The light obviously radiates - but a large proportion of that is in the visible spectrum, which (to my limited knowledge) doesn't produce a lot of heat. What little heat (from combustion) is produced by the candle/wick then heats the immediately surrounding air molecules and that, by convection, is dissipated very quickly with little heating effect on objects a few feet or metres away - and eventually ends up heating cold, dark/draughty corners of the room - or usually, any object placed just overhead the candle/wick. Rob's point is that, by trapping all of the BTUs with a "device" (I believe Rob would call it a "Converter") which converts the would-be convected heat into energy which heats the "pot" (or saucepan) - actually, a "proper" radiator, then the converted energy is now travelling on into the universe (room) as radiated energy - which will be transferred by the frequencies such as infra-red - which in turn cause heating by radiation - rather than convection. It's the difference between warm air currents wafting "willy-nilly" around a room (and maybe heating some parts a bit) and radiation energy - which is transmitted in straight lines, effecting thermal change on whatever those rays hit first. I might be wrong and apologise for my ignorance but... I think that's the point of the whole thing? I'm gonna test this out myself... :-)

text: Wouldn't the plant pot heater radiate more heat if it was painted with black stove paint?

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Nah, color is just how it reflects light. That only matters if it needs to bounce its own rays off of itself, like radiator fins, because they face each other. White is probably what you want there, although the optical color doesn't really matter, it's all about infrared. Plant pots and sauce pans are totally convex, so only the surface temperature matters. For an object emitting rays because it's hot, that's called black body radiation (ironically). There's a formula for the spectrum of light emitted at any given temperature, and the material is totally irrelevant. If you ever go shopping for light bulbs and they have a color temperature in thousands of degrees, that's what it means. Now if you have a plasma, like a star or the Sun, there will also be spectral emission lines from the specific elements, but most of the light is just from heat. If you want to go down a physics and science history rabbit hole related to all this, search "ultraviolet catastrophe".

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: Actually after reading some more, I might have to correct myself here. There is a certain emissivity for each material that affects how ideal of a black body radiator it is. So there might be something to it after all. I guess the best way to tell is try it and see.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: @DFPercush   You'e correct that the color doesn't matter, but what you don't want, is a shiny metallic surface, because that reflects IR better. Painting the outside of the pot will increase its emissivity i.e. make it more transparent to IR.

author: @Ghost_Os
text: I grew up in a location which routinely saw winter temperatures drop to minus 60F - real temperature, not windchill. We heated our entire house with a stone woodstove. No ducts, no fans, we didn't even have electricity. It was quite warm and snug, even sixty feet away on the other side of a wall (single story dwelling). Radiant heat, of course, results in convective heating as well, since as the air is warmed, it's set into motion. I would even argue that there's a sort of conductive heating that takes place beyond the stove itself - as things are heated which are in the path of the radiation, they become warm (or quite hot as was the case with an old steel refrigerator that we used for rodent-free food storage 😆 ); the heat conducts along that object, and of course at a certain point, these passive objects begin to radiate heat themselves. So in that sense, radiative heat behaves quite a bit like vibration with a tuning fork. Interesting to think about it in that way.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: I saw a tour of a little house in Siberia one time. The whole thing was build around a stone wood stove. Pretty much a one room house with a loft. Makes a lot of sense. Stone retains heat for a long time after the fire dies down.

replies.author: @Ghost_Os

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: I don't think vegetable oil has too many toxic chemicals. Motor oil probably does. The main concern is carbon monoxide. I'm pretty sure kerosene and parrafin can be used indoors, and of course there's natural gas (methane) or propane. Both produce CO2, but you have to make sure the flame has plenty of oxygen, because incomplete combustion can produce CO, not good. You can actually burn CO, that's a majority of what's in wood gas. It will release energy and form CO2, but it tends to have a hard time sustaining its own flame. There needs to be something else burning with it. I would suggest alcohol, but I think it's pretty much the same story with any carbon based fuel. Alcohol is basically a hydrocarbon with an extra oxygen atom. As long as it burns clean it's fine. The extra oxygen in the molecule might help with that, but that's literally just a random thought I had just now, and I've done zero research about that. But a carbon monoxide alarm is good to have any time you have a flame that's not exhausted to the outside. The only other thing I can think of would be to throw some aluminum foil in acid and make hydrogen, but that's very dangerous and expensive. I think you can burn ammonia, that produces nitrogen and water. It's only toxic before you burn it, and if you don't burn all of it you'll definitely know it lol. Only problem is the stuff you buy from the store is mostly water, and I think that's ammonium hydroxide, not actual ammonia gas. It has a somewhat reasonable melting point for cryogenic storage, just barely above the temperature of dry ice. But if you ever lose cooling you better have a way to vent it. I'd stay away from it personally.

replies.author: @InclusiveDriving
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering if you stand naked in direct sunlight when it's -1°C outside, I suspect hypothermia will take hold, even with the substantial amount of energy from the sun.

My concern with radiated heat (including halogen heaters) is down to behaviour. This is purely speculation on my part, but radiated heat activates your body's thermoreceptors, making you feel warm. But unless you heat the surrounding air by convection, there is still a high temperature gradient, meaning the human body will lose heat without feeling cold.

author: @misamsung6191
text: The cooking pot will radiate heat more quickly than the plant pot.  But with greater thermal mass the plant pot will radiate heat longer. Therein is the trade off quicker heating up versus longer radiating time.  I wonder what would happen if one used a cooking pot that has a greater thermal mass such as a cast iron cooking pot or Dutch Oven.   Logic would indicate that using a heavy cooking pot will take a bit longer to heat up than a plant pot but will continue to radiate heat longer than a steel or aluminium cooking pot.
